coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-modified TiO2 nanoparticles for antibacterial applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Honghui
Zhang, Jie
Gao, Jian
Liang, Ziyu
Li, Wei
Yan, Hong
Guo, Ruijie
Wang, Huifang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by top-down hydrothermal method by corn stalks as a carbon source. The CQDs were composited with titanium dioxide (TiO2) to improve its antibacterial properties under visible light. The prepared nanocomposites were characterized by UV–Vis, EA, PL, TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. PL analysis showed that the prepared CQDs had good upconversion fluorescence properties. FTIR and XPS demonstrated that the CQDs were successfully loaded onto the surface of TiO2, which were connected by C-O-Ti bonds. Finally, the effect of CQDs on the photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties of TiO2 was evaluated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Titanium dioxide
Photocatalysis
Antibacterial

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible absorption spectrum of CQDs solutions (a), photographs of CQDs solutions under natural light (b) and 365 nm UV irradiation (c).
Downconversion (a) and upconversion (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs.
TEM images of CQDs (a) and CQDs /TiO2 NPs (b), particle size distribution of CQDs (c), HRTEM images of CQDs (d) and CQDs /TiO2 NPs (e).
(a) XRD patterns of CQDs and CQDs /TiO2 NPs; Fig. 4(b) FTIR spectra of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs /TiO2 NPs.
XPS full spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NPs (a), XPS spectra of C 1 s (b), O 1 s (c) and Ti 2p (d) energy levels.
Performance of CQDs/TiO2 NPs degrading MB with different CQDs contents: degradation curves in the dark (a), degradation curves in the UV(b), degradation curves in the visible light (c).
Schematic diagram of CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic reaction.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-modified TiO2 nanoparticles for antibacterial applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by top-down hydrothermal method by corn stalks as a carbon source. The CQDs were composited with titanium dioxide (TiO2) to improve its antibacterial properties under visible light. The prepared nanocomposites were characterized by UV–Vis, EA, PL, TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. PL analysis showed that the prepared CQDs had good upconversion fluorescence properties. FTIR and XPS demonstrated that the CQDs were successfully loaded onto the surface of TiO2, which were connected by C-O-Ti bonds. Finally, the effect of CQDs on the photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties of TiO2 was evaluated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Targeting microbial contamination has given rise to a wide range of antimicrobial materials [1,2]. Among these, photocatalytic antimicrobial materials represent a particularly active area of research. The photocatalytic bacteriostatic materials are inorganic materials utilizing photocatalytic action to eliminate bacteria. Such materials are typically environment friendly and nontoxic. They often exhibit excellent antimicrobial properties and are less likely to be resistant to chemicals. Research on photocatalytic antibacterial materials is gaining attention [3].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) have become popular photocatalytic antimicrobial materials in recent years due to its unique electron structure, chemical stability, high oxidative capacity, non-toxicity, and inexpensive [4,5]. The photocatalytic properties of TiO2 are attributed to the UV excitation of electron conduction band (CB) jumps in the valence band (VB), which leads to the formation of electrons and holes. The electrons and holes will react with moisture and oxygen on the TiO2 surface to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) radicals such as HO− and O2−. These radicals can oxidizing organic molecules and microorganisms on the TiO2 surface, achieving bacterial inactivation [6,7]. However, the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of pure TiO2 is limited to UV irradiation, which represents only 5 % of the solar spectrum. Consequently, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 has significant limitations under natural light [8]. Furthermore, pure TiO2 have inherent defects, such as large bandgap and carrier complexes. Therefore, it is necessary to improve the photocatalytic performance of pure TiO2 under visible light.

Modifying the energy-band structure of TiO2 by assembling it into various carbon materials is receiving increased research attention. Among them, fluorescence-based quantum dots (QDs) are eliciting significant interest in this field because of their tunable photovoltaic properties [9]. Green synthesized CQDs using an environmentally friendly carbon source make up for the shortcomings of conventional QDs in terms of environmental protection. Regarding photophysical properties, CQDs exhibit excellent upconversion photoluminescence and photostability [10]. Numerous studies have been carried out on the modification of TiO2 with CQDs. Nevertheless, the majority of reported CQDs with excellent photocatalytic performance are attributed to the consumption of environmentally harmful substances [11]. Therefore, the eco-friendly synthesis of CQDs using natural resources is gaining significant attention.

In the present study, we recommend a green and economically efficient method to improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 with CQDs. CQDs were prepared by a simple and effective in situ hydrothermal technique and then deposited onto the TiO2 surface. The content of CQDs was changed to determine its effect on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2.

In this study, corn stalk was purchased from Lian Feng Agricultural Products Co. Ltd., titanium dioxide (TiO2, AR) from Degussa, anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, AR) from Shanghai McLean Co. Ltd., and methylene blue (MB, C16H18N3ClS, AR) from Shanghai Aladdin Co. Ltd. Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 8099) was purchased from Tianjin DaMao Chemical Reagent Factory, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 6538) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Co. Ltd., and nutrient broth (biochemical reagent) was purchased from Shanghai McLean Co. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Adopted a top-down synthesis approach, the CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using corn stalk as a carbon source. Corn stalk was pre-crushed in a pulverizer for 30 s. Measure 1 g of corn stalk powder into 50 ml of deionized water and stir for 30 min, add to the reactor and seal. The reaction was carried out by heating at 180 °C for 10 h in an electrically heated blast dryer. The reaction was carried out in an electrically heated oven. After the solution in the reactor was cooled and sonicated for 1 h, the solution was extracted and filtered to take the clear solution, the clear night was centrifuged (6000 rpm, 10 min), and then the upper layer of the clear solution was taken out and freeze-dried to obtain CQDs.

To prepare the CQDs/TiO2 NPs by hydrothermal method, the steps were as follows: TiO2 (100 mg) were added into 50 ml of anhydrous ethanol and ultrasonicated for 15 min, and different contents of CQDs (mass fractions of 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, and 4 % of TiO2) were added, respectively. The mixed solution was put into a reactor and heated to 150 °C for 8 h. After the solution was cooled down, it was centrifuged several times (6000 rpm, 10 min) and washed several times using deionized water and alcohol, and the precipitates were placed in a vacuum oven and dried at 90 °C overnight, and finally the CQDs/TiO2 NPs were obtained by grinding.

The elemental composition of corn stover and CQDs were determined using an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) operating at an acceleration voltage of 15 kV. The optical properties of the samples were determined using an UV spectrophotometer (UV1800, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, F-4700, Hitachi) was used to detect the photoluminescence of the CQDs solution at 325 nm. The morphology of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs was recorded using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos L120C, Thermo Fisher, USA). The crystal structure of the samples was examined using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 VENTURE, BrukerCorporation, Germany). The functional groups on the surface of the samples were analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 380 American Thermoelectric USA). The elemental composition and molecular structure of the samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Nexsa, Thermo Fisher, USA). The quantum yield(Φ) was calculated from Eq. 1:
(1)
Φ
%
=
W 1
W 2
× 100 %
where W 1 is the number of photons emitted and W 2 is the number of photons absorbed.

At room temperature, 50 mg of the product was dispersed in 100 mL of MB solution at a concentration of 10 ppm. After stirring for 1 h in the dark, the MB solution was tested for degradation rate under UV light. The same conditions were used to simulate solar irradiation with a 1000 W halogen lamp (λ > 450 nm) with a cut-off filter. The solution containing MB dye was continuously stirred under irradiation for 50 h, and 6 mL of the sample solution was collected every 2 h. After centrifugation, the concentration of MB in solution was determined at a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and the photocatalytic degradation rate of MB was calculated.

The antibacterial activity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs was analyzed by the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) method. Different concentrations of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 NPs solutions were prepared using the twofold dilution method. Take 50uL of the bacterial solution with a concentration of 1 × 107 CFU/mL and add it to the above solutions respectively. The OD values of the bacterial solutions at 600 nm were recorded using an enzyme immunoassay detector after 24 h in a water bath shaker.

The elemental compositions of corn stover and CQDs are listed in Table S1. Distinctive X-ray peaks corresponding to multiple elements were identified, encompassing carbon (C), oxygen (O), sodium (Na), magnesium (Mg), silicon (Si), sulfur (S), chloride (Cl), potassium (K), and calcium (Ca). The presence and distribution of these elements are intrinsically linked to the biological functions and structural integrity of corn stover. Moreover, this elemental composition offers critical insights for leveraging corn stover in diverse fields such as agriculture, environmental science, and material science.

The elemental composition analysis of CQDs reveals that C and O were the most abundant elements, collectively accounting for approximately 94.75 % of the total composition. This finding indicated that the surface of the prepared CQDs was rich in carbon- and oxygen-containing functional groups [12]. These functional groups provided active sites for the participation various substances and facilitated the functionalization of CQDs [13]. CQDs also contained small amounts of Na, Mg, Si, S, Cl, and K elements. This result may be due to the use of corn stalk as a carbon source to synthesize CQDs through a top-down approach. Some inorganic materials in the corn stalk were also stripped off [14]. Notably, the relative concentration of trace elements in the CQDs exhibited a significant increase compared to the original corn stover feedstock. This phenomenon was principally ascribed to thermochemical transformations, including carbonization and oxidative decomposition of organic constituents in corn stover during hydrothermal processing. These reactions lead to significant organic matter depletion and subsequent relative accumulation of inorganic components. Furthermore, the unique high-temperature, high-pressure conditions characteristic of hydrothermal processing likely promoted the dissolution and subsequent recrystallization of specific inorganic ions, thereby enhancing their incorporation into the CQDs matrix [15].

The UV absorption spectrum of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1(a). The peak at 279 nm corresponded to the π-π* transition of sp.2 hybridized CC bonds, while the peak at 326 nm arose from the n-π* transition of CO bonds in the CQDs structure [16]. These peaks confirmed the presence of conjugated aromatic systems and oxygen-containing groups, which critically influenced the optical properties of CQDs, such as absorption and fluorescence. The CQDs solution appeared tan when exposed to visible light (Fig. 1(b)) and exhibited blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1(c)). The luminescence properties demonstrated the successful preparation of CQDs using corn stalk. N-doping CQDs prepared by Zeng et al. from waste oil palm leaves showed similar fluorescence properties [17].

The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a) shows the red shift of the corresponding emission peak from 382 nm to 522 nm with increased excitation wavelength from 250 nm to 500 nm. In this case, CQDs exhibited typical downconversion fluorescence properties [18]. The strength of the downconversion emission peaks tends to increase as the excitation wavelength increased. The intensity of the emitted light was maximized when the excitation wavelength was 300 nm. With further increased excitation wavelength to 500 nm, the luminescence intensity decreased. This apparent excitation light-dependent behavior revealed the tunable emission property of CQDs [19,20].

Fig. 2(b) shows that the CQDs also exhibited good upconversion fluorescence properties when the excitation wavelength was 550–800 nm. With increased excitation wavelength, the emission peaks of CQDs appeared between 341 and 482 nm. The upconversion fluorescence properties of CQDs also exhibited excitation light-dependent behavior. The excitation wavelength energy of upconversion fluorescence was lower than the emission wavelength energy compared to downconversion fluorescence. This revealed that the upconversion fluorescence property of CQDs allowed them to absorb visible and near-infrared light and convert it to shorter wavelengths. This property of CQDs allowed them to be used as an energy-conversion component. TiO2 was well documented to exhibit a low utilization of visible light, thereby restricting its photocatalytic capacity. The composite of CQDs with TiO2 was expected to improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light. The reason was that the CQDs irradiated with visible light and converted it into the more energetic UV light. The UV light then induced the electrons in the VB of TiO2 to migrate toward the CB and stimulated the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [21]. Ultimately, efficient utilization of TiO2 for visible light was achieved. Liu et al. exhibited the same upconversion fluorescence properties of CQDs extracted from glucose as a carbon source and successfully enhanced the photocatalytic performance of P25 under visible light [22]. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ), representing a crucial parameter for evaluating the luminescence efficiency of fluorescent materials. The Φ of CQDs was determined to be 17 % using Rhodamine B as the reference standard. This might be due to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs served as non-radiative complex centers, which in turn affected the Φ. Moreover, the presence of trace elements in CQDs might introduce additional energy levels or defect states, thereby influencing their luminescence properties [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs are shown in Fig. 3(a) and Fig. 3(b). The particle-size distribution of CQDs is shown in Fig. 3(c). Fig. 3(a) shows that the synthesized CQDs are uniform in size and well dispersed. Fig. 3(b) shows that the TiO2 in the CQDs/TiO2 NPs is in the form of rectangular flakes. Numerous uniformly dark spots were scattered among the rectangular pieces. Notably, the nanoparticles overlapped with one another. This finding indicated that a tight interfacial contact had been established between the CQDs and TiO2 [17,24]. Fig. 3(c) reveals that the average particle size of CQDs was 2.23 ± 0.34 nm.

The HRTEM of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs are shown in Fig. 3(d) and Fig. 3(e). Fig. 3(d) shows that the CQDs had a clear lattice stripe of 0.215 nm. This stripe corresponded with the (100) crystal plane of the graphite structure [25]. Lattice streaks belonging to TiO2 rutile and anatase could be observed from Fig. 3(e). Where the lattice spacing of 0.32 and 0.35 nm corresponded with the (110) crystal plane of rutile and the (101) crystal plane of anatase, respectively [26]. Lattice fringes belonging to CQDs are also observed in Fig. 3(e), indicating that the CQDs were successfully compounded onto TiO2.

The XRD spectra of CQDs, pure TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2 NPs are shown in Fig. 4(a). The XRD analysis revealed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 22° for the CQDs, which corresponds to the (002) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon, typically exhibiting an crystalline facet spacing of 0.32–0.34 nm. However, the crystalline facet spacing calculated from our XRD data (0.403 nm) showed a slight expansion compared to the standard graphite value. This deviation could be attributed to the presence of abundant polar functional groups on the CQDs surfaces. Bibekananda et al. demonstrated that the variation in crystal plane spacing was influenced by the functional groups present on the surface of carbon dots [27]. The incorporation of these surface functional groups enhances the amorphous characteristics of the carbon dots, consequently resulting in the expansion of the crystalline facet spacing. The XRD spectrum of pure TiO2 showed peaks at 2θ = 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.7°, and 75.0°, corresponding with anatase (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1), (2 0 4), and (2 1 5) crystal planes [28]. The diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 27.4°, 36.1°, 41.2°, 54.3°, 56.6°, and 69.0° corresponded with the rutile (1 1 0), (1 0 1), (1 1 1), (2 1 1), (2 2 0), and (3 0 1) crystal planes [29]. The CQDs/TiO2 NPs exhibited characteristic peaks similar to those of pure TiO2. As a result, pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 NPs presented a mixed phase of anatase and rutile. Furthermore, the XRD spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NPs exhibited a weak characteristic peak of CQDs at 23° [27,30,31]. XRD analysis indicated that CQDs wouldn't change the crystal structure and particle size of TiO2.

The FTIR spectra of CQDs, TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2 NPs are shown in Fig. 4(b). The FTIR spectrum of CQDs showed an absorption peak of OH stretched vibration at 3425 cm−1. The vibrational peaks of CH and CO appeared at 2925 and 1635 cm-1 [32]. The stretching vibrational peaks of COC and CO appeared at 1261 and 1055 cm−1. This finding indicated that the surface of CQDs possessed abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. These functional groups facilitated the anchoring of CQDs onto the TiO2 surface during hydrothermal processes [33]. Besides, the CQDs spectrum showed a CC absorption peak at 1456 cm−1. This possibly attributed to the formation of conjugated double bonds by the carbonization of corn stalk during the hydrothermal process [34]. In the FTIR spectrum of pure TiO2, the peaks at 3425 and 1635 cm−1 were correlated with the OH stretching and bending vibrations of water adsorbed onto the sample surface. The broad absorption peak observed at 500–700 cm−1 was attributed to the TiOTi bond. The FTIR spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NPs exhibited COC and CO stretching vibrational peaks at 1261 and 1055 cm−1 compared with pure TiO2 [35]. Moreover, the broad absorption peaks of CQDs/TiO2 NPs at 500–700 cm−1 shifted. This might be a result of the presence of the COTi vibrational peak. Sang et al. constructed graphene oxide/TiO2 through in situ photochemical reduction and found that the shift of the absorption peaks at similar positions might be assigned to the superposition of the COTi and TiOTi vibrational peaks [36]. This result suggested the formation of a chemical bond between CQDs and TiO2.

The XPS spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NPs is shown in Fig. 5 . The fully measured spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NPs is shown in Fig. 5(a). Among them, the peaks located at 285.08, 477.69, and 535.44 eV were attributed to C1s, Ti2p, and O1s, respectively. This finding confirmed that CQDs were successfully composited with TiO2. Fig. 5(b) shows the plot of Ti2p after peak splitting. The peaks at 458.7 and 464.4 eV corresponded with Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively. The splitting energy between them was 5.7 eV, indicating that Ti4+ was the dominant state of Ti in CQDs/TiO2 NPs, consistent with literature [37]. Fig. 5(c) shows the plot of O1s after deconvolution. The absorption peaks for TiO, CO and COH bonds were observed at 529.8 eV, 530.6 eV and 531.9 eV, respectively [38]. This revealed that the CQDs were successfully attached onto the TiO2 surface. The plot of C1s after deconvolution is shown in Fig. 5(d). The peaks of CC/CC, CO, and COTi bonds appeared at 284.7 eV, 286.23 eV, and 288.6 eV, respectively [39]. The CQDs were connected with TiO2 via COTi bonds. The results of XPS analysis were consistent with those of FTIR analysis.

To investigate the effects of CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs was tested using methylene blue solution (MB) to simulate the pollutants. Fig. 6 shows the degradation of MB by CQDs/TiO2 NPs at different CQDs contents. The degradation curves of CQDs/TiO2 NPs on MB solution under dark conditions are shown in Fig. 6(a). After 120 min, the other four groups of samples showed small changes in absorbance in darkness. It may be that the material reached an adsorption–desorption equilibrium state, revealing that CQDs/TiO2 NPs had no photocatalytic activity in darkness.

The degradation profile of CQDs/TiO2 NPs on MB solution under UV irradiation is shown in Fig. 6(b). The degradation of MB solution by pure TiO2 reached 78.3 % after 30 min of light exposure. As the content of CQDs increased, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 NPs improved. When the CQDs content reached 2 wt%, the degradation efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 NPs for MB was the highest. After 30 min of UV irradiation, the degradation rate of MB reached 95.1 %. This finding was attributed to the fact that electron-hole complexation was the main factor limiting the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. The introduced CQDs were capable of providing surface channels for electrons in the TiO2 CB [24]. This process enhanced the efficiency of electrons and holes to separate, which subsequently improved the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. Interestingly, when the CQDs content was increased to 3 wt% and 4 wt%, a decline in the degradation efficiency of MB by CQDs/TiO2 NPs was observed, with degradation rates dropping to 71.1 % and 70.0 %, respectively. This reduction was likely attributable to the agglomeration of excess CQDs, which hindered their catalytic effectiveness. Nevertheless, despite the diminished photocatalytic performance, the degradation efficiencies of NPs with varying CQDs contents consistently surpassed those of pure TiO2.

The degradation curves of CQDs/TiO2 NPs on MB solution under simulated sunlight conditions are shown in Fig. 6(c). The degradation of MB solution by pure TiO2 was about 40.9 % after 20 h of light exposure. In contrast, all CQDs/TiO2 NPs embedded with varying CQDs contents demonstrated significantly enhanced photocatalytic activities after 20 h of visible light irradiation. The best photocatalytic performance of the complexes was achieved at 2 % CQDs content, and the degradation rate can reach 95.2 %. This implied that CQDs could obviously improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 in the visible light. At this point, the upconversion fluorescence property allows CQDs to convert visible or near-infrared light into UV light [40]. The substantial quantity of UV light prompted TiO2 to fully exploit its photocatalytic properties, thereby facilitating the dye degradation. However, similar to the UV degradation results, the catalytic efficiency of NPs under visible light also exhibited a noticeable decline when the CQDs content reached 3 wt% and 4 wt%, with degradation rates dropping to 75.5 % and 65.6 %, respectively. This might be due to the excess CQDs pre-empting the photoresponsive sites on the TiO2 surface, leading to decreased in the degradation efficiency. Pan et al. prepared TiO2 nanotubes composited with CQDs. They also observed a decrease in photocatalytic efficiency when the content of CQDs is excessive [41].

Fig. 7 shows a schematic diagram of the photocatalytic reaction of CQDs with TiO2. The reactions occurring in the degradation process are shown in Eqs. (3-1)–(3-5).
(3-1)
Ti
O 2
+ h v →
e −
+
h +
(3-2)
e −
+
O 2
→ ·
O 2 −
(3-3)
h +
+
H 2
O / O
H −
→ · OH
(3-4)
·
O 2 −
+ · OH → ROS
(3-5)
ROS + MB → C
O 2
+
H 2
O

When the CQDs/TiO2 NPs were irradiated, the CQDs converted visible light into shorter wavelength light (325–425 nm). This stimulated the electrons in the VB of TiO2 to jump to the CB and leave holes in the VB. When electrons and holes migrated onto the TiO2 surface, electron/hole pairs formed. The reaction of electron/hole pairs with moisture or oxygen molecules adsorbed on the material surface produced ROS. The high chemical reactivity of the ROS caused the MB molecules to break down into harmless substances. The energy-band edges of CQDs can also be used for electron transfer. This process facilitated electron/hole separation and stabilization [42].

Dye-degradation experiments indicated that CQDs improved the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 in the visible light. The strong oxidizing activity of ROS was found to facilitate the inhibition of bacteria [43]. Accordingly, the antimicrobial activity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs was tested. The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of CQDs/TiO2 NPs in visible light are listed in Table 1 . The MICs values of CQDs/TiO2 NPs were 1200, 150, 200, 300, and 400 ppm at a certain concentration of E. coli solution with 0 %, 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, and 4 % of CQDs, respectively. The MIC values for the corresponding samples in S. aureus solution were 800, 150, 100, 200 and 200 ppm. The optimal inhibition of E. coli by TiO2 was achieved when the CQDs content was 1 %. At this time, the MIC value was 150 ppm. TiO2 with 2 % CQDs showed the most pronounced inhibition of S. aureus. The MIC value was 100 ppm. Antimicrobial tests demonstrated that CQDs enhanced the antimicrobial properties of TiO2 in visible light. This finding revealed that the upconversion property of CQDs led to more ROS production from TiO2. When ROS come into contact with the cell membrane of bacteria, the oxidative stress of ROS disrupts their cell-membrane structure. Furthermore, ROS also inflicts oxidative damage to intracellular nucleotides, lipids, and proteins, resulting in the death and breakdown of bacteria [44]. However, the antimicrobial activity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs exhibited a tendency of increasing and decreasing with increased of CQDs content. The decrease in antimicrobial activity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs could be explained by the high content of CQDs, which caused agglomeration and affected the separation efficiency of the electron/hole. Yan et al. prepared CQDs using graphite electrodes, and the TiO2 modified with excessive CQDs showed the same decrease in antibacterial ability under visible light [45].

In summary, a simple hydrothermal method was used to prepare CQDs/TiO2 NPs. PL analysis showed that CQDs exerted good upconversion fluorescence effects. Lattice fringes belonging to CQDs were observed in the TEM patterns of CQDs/TiO2 NPs. The XRD spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 NPs showed a weak characteristic peak of CQDs at 22°. FTIR and XPS results demonstrated that the CQDs were successfully complexed on the TiO2 surface, which were connected by C-O-Ti bonds. MB dye-degradation experiments indicated that the upconversion fluorescence property of CQDs enabled TiO2 to produce more ROS in visible light. The best inhibition of E. coli was achieved by TiO2 when the CQDs content was 1 % with a MIC value of 150 ppm. The most significant inhibitory effect of TiO2 on S. aureus was observed at a CQDs content of 2 %. The MIC value was 100 ppm. Ultimately, the proposed CQDs/TiO2 NPs preparation method was cost-effective, and green. The NPs showed significant advantages in photocatalytic degradation and good antimicrobial properties.

Honghui Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Jie Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jian Gao: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Ziyu Liang: Visualization, Software, Investigation. Wei Li: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Hong Yan: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ruijie Guo: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources. Huifang Wang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
Ethics approval is not required for this research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22178242) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (202103021 224101).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2025.142055.
